title: Add Partial Products
description: Add the partial products from each place-value multiplication to obtain the final product.
condition: Given two or more partial products from multi-digit multiplication
response: Add the partial products accurately using place-value alignment
label: Principle / Model
example: After finding $3,140$ and $12,560$ from $628 \times 25$, compute $3,140 + 12,560 = 15,700$.
notes: Requires understanding of place-value alignment during addition.

title: Align Digits by Place Value
description: Align digits according to place value when writing numbers and partial products in the standard algorithm.
condition: Writing multi-digit factors and partial products vertically
response: Align digits so that ones, tens, hundreds, etc., are in the same columns
label: Category
example: When writing $257$ above $63$, align the $7$ and $3$ in the ones column.
notes: Often implicit in written work; supports correct computation.

title: Area of a Rectangle by Multiplication
description: Compute the area of a rectangular region by multiplying its length and width.
condition: A rectangle with given length and width measurements.
response: Multiply length × width to find area in square units.
label: Principle / Model
example: For a rectangle 660 km by 343 km, area = $660 \times 343$ square kilometers.

title: Commutative Property of Multiplication
description: Multiplication remains true when the order of the factors is reversed.
condition: A true multiplication equation of the form a × b = c is given.
response: Recognize or state that b × a = c is also true.
label: Principle / Model

title: Compare Product to Benchmark Value
description: Determine whether a computed or estimated product is greater than or less than a given benchmark number.
condition: A product (exact or estimated) and a benchmark value (e.g., 200,000).
response: State whether the product is greater than or less than the benchmark and justify using comparison.
label: Principle / Model
example: Since $600 \times 300 = 180{,}000$ and $600 \times 40 = 24{,}000$, the total is more than $200{,}000$.

title: Compare Volumes via Common Factors
description: Compare two volumes by identifying common factors and comparing remaining products.
condition: Two volume expressions sharing a common dimension (e.g., both include a factor of 8).
response: Factor out the common dimension and compare the remaining products to determine which volume is larger.
label: Principle / Model
example: Since both have 8, compare $23 \times 12 = 276$ with $40 \times 9 = 360$.

title: Decompose Factor Using Partial Products
description: Break apart a factor into place-value components to compute or estimate a product.
condition: A multi-digit multiplication expression (e.g., 600 × 340).
response: Rewrite one factor as a sum (e.g., 340 = 300 + 40) and multiply each part, then combine results.
label: Principle / Model

title: Determine Number of Loads by Volume Comparison
description: Determine how many equal loads are needed to fill a larger volume by comparing or dividing volumes and interpreting the result.
condition: A smaller unit volume and a larger total volume to be filled.
response: Divide or repeatedly compare volumes and round up to the next whole number if needed.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one load is $23 \times 8 \times 12 = 2{,}208$ ft³ and container is $40 \times 8 \times 9 = 2{,}880$ ft³, then $2{,}208 < 2{,}880$, so 2 loads are needed.
notes: Requires interpreting remainder in context (cannot have fractional load).

title: Estimate Quotient Using Place Value
description: Estimate a division result by reasoning about place value and nearby benchmark numbers.
condition: A division expression with large whole numbers is given and an estimate is requested.
response: Use place value reasoning or compatible numbers to determine the closest power-of-ten estimate.
label: Principle / Model
example: $8{,}745 \div 3$ is about $9{,}000 \div 3 = 3{,}000$, so the closest choice is $3{,}000$.

title: Evaluate Reasonableness Using Estimation
description: Use estimation to determine whether a claimed product is reasonable.
condition: Given a multiplication expression and an estimated or claimed product
response: Round one or both factors, compute an approximate product, and compare to the claim
label: Principle / Model
example: To evaluate $257 \times 63 \approx 1,500$, round to $200 \times 60 = 12,000$, which is far greater than $1,500$, so the claim is unreasonable.
notes: Focuses on place-value reasoning when estimating.

title: Execute Standard Long Division Algorithm
description: Carry out the standard long division steps: divide, multiply, subtract, and bring down digits until the remainder is zero or less than the divisor.
condition: Given a multi-digit dividend and a one- or two-digit divisor in long division format.
response: Iteratively divide, multiply, subtract, and bring down digits to compute the quotient and remainder.
label: Production / Skill
example: Compute $966 \div 23$ by dividing 96 by 23 (4), subtracting $92$, bringing down 6, dividing 46 by 23 (2), yielding $42$.

title: Find Width from Area and Length
description: Given the area of a rectangle and one side length, compute the unknown side by dividing area by the known length.
condition: Area of a rectangle and one side length are given; the other side length is unknown.
response: Divide the area by the known side length to find the missing side length.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the area is $5{,}063$ square feet and the length is $61$ feet, then width $= 5{,}063 \div 61 = 83$ feet.

title: Interpret Exact Quotient
description: Recognize when division results in no remainder and state the quotient as a whole number.
condition: After completing division where the remainder equals 0.
response: State the quotient as the final answer with no remainder.
label: Concept
example: Since $966 \div 23 = 42$ with remainder $0$, the value is $42$.

title: Interpret Remainder in Division
description: Recognize and state the remainder when a dividend is not evenly divisible by the divisor.
condition: A division calculation results in a nonzero remainder.
response: State the quotient with the remainder or interpret the leftover amount.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Rationale marked No because remainder notation is conventional.

title: Inverse Relationship of Multiplication and Division
description: Understand that division can be used to undo multiplication and verify quotients.
condition: Given a division expression or a multiplication equation with a missing factor.
response: Use multiplication to check division or division to find a missing factor.
label: Principle / Model
example: Since $23 \times 42 = 966$, it follows that $966 \div 23 = 42$.
notes: Central conceptual goal of the assessment.

title: Multiply by Ones Digit First
description: In the standard algorithm, begin multiplication with the ones digit of the multiplier.
condition: Given a vertical multi-digit multiplication problem using the standard algorithm
response: Multiply the entire top number by the ones digit of the bottom number first
label: Concept
example: For $359 \times 63$, first multiply $359 \times 3$ before multiplying by $6$ (which represents $60$).
notes: Procedural convention of the standard U.S. algorithm.

title: Multiply by Tens Digit (Shift Left)
description: When multiplying by a tens digit, shift the partial product one place to the left (or append a zero) to reflect place value.
condition: In the standard algorithm, multiplying a multi-digit number by the tens digit of the multiplier
response: Write the partial product starting in the tens place (or append one zero)
label: Principle / Model
example: For $628 \times 25$, when multiplying by $2$ (which represents $20$), compute $628 \times 20 = 12,560$, writing the product shifted one place left.
notes: Connects digit position to value (e.g., 2 in 25 represents 20).

title: Multiply Multi-Digit Numbers
description: Use the standard algorithm or partial products to multiply multi-digit whole numbers.
condition: Two multi-digit whole numbers are given to be multiplied.
response: Compute the product using place value and multiplication steps.
label: Production / Skill
example: $213 \times 54 = 11{,}502$ using partial products: $213 \times 50 + 213 \times 4$.

title: Multiply Single Digits with Regrouping
description: Multiply two digits and regroup (carry) when the product is 10 or greater.
condition: Multiplying two base-10 digits where the product is 10 or more
response: Write the ones digit and carry the tens digit to the next place
label: Principle / Model
example: When multiplying $8 \times 5 = 40$, write $0$ in the ones place and carry $4$ to the tens place.
notes: Relies on place-value structure of base-10 system.

title: Round Numbers to Estimate Products
description: Round factors to nearby friendly numbers to estimate a product for reasoning or comparison.
condition: A multiplication situation where an estimate is sufficient.
response: Round one or more factors to convenient values and multiply to estimate.
label: Principle / Model
example: South Dakota is about $600 \times 300 = 180{,}000$, so $200{,}000$ is a reasonable estimate.

title: Use Division to Find a Missing Factor
description: Find an unknown factor by dividing the product by the known factor(s).
condition: Given a multiplication equation with one unknown factor (e.g., $a \times b = c$) and values for $b$ and $c$.
response: Compute $a = c \div b$ (or divide by the product of known factors).
label: Principle / Model
example: Given $14 \times 7 \times w = 1{,}176$, first compute $14 \times 7 = 98$, then $w = 1{,}176 \div 98 = 12$.
notes: Relies on inverse relationship between multiplication and division.

title: Use Partial Quotients Strategy
description: Decompose the dividend using large, convenient multiples of the divisor and sum the partial quotients.
condition: Given a division problem where the divisor can be multiplied by place-value-friendly numbers (e.g., tens).
response: Subtract large multiples of the divisor successively and add the partial quotients.
label: Rule / Plan
example: For $966 \div 23$: subtract $23 \times 20 = 460$ twice (total 40), then $23 \times 2 = 46$, giving $42$.

title: Volume of a Rectangular Prism
description: Use the formula for volume of a rectangular prism as the product of its three dimensions.
condition: Given length, width, and height of a rectangular prism.
response: Compute volume using $V = l \times w \times h$.
label: Principle / Model
